title: Pancreatic Cancer
oneLiner: Aggressive ductal adenocarcinoma of the exocrine pancreas; presents late with painless jaundice, weight loss — 5-year survival <10%.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 72-year-old man with a 30-pack-year smoking history and recently diagnosed type 2 diabetes presents with two weeks of progressive jaundice, pruritus, dark urine, and pale stools. He has lost 8 kg unintentionally over three months. On exam, he is icteric with a palpable, non-tender gallbladder (Courvoisier's sign). No fever. LFTs show obstructive picture (bilirubin 120 µmol/L, ALP 450 U/L). CT abdomen reveals a hypodense mass in the pancreatic head with dilated common bile duct and pancreatic duct (“double-duct sign”).

**Dissect the clues:**
- **Painless jaundice + palpable gallbladder** → Courvoisier’s law: in jaundice, a palpable gallbladder is likely malignant (pancreatic cancer) rather than stones (gallbladder is fibrotic/scarred in cholecystitis).
- **Unintentional weight loss** → cachexia from tumor cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) and early satiety from duodenal compression or gastroparesis.
- **New-onset diabetes** → paraneoplastic effect; tumor secretes amylin, causes insulin resistance; also loss of β-cell mass.
- **Smoking history** → strongest modifiable risk factor.
- **Double-duct sign on CT** → pathognomonic for pancreatic head mass obstructing both pancreatic and common bile ducts.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Back pain alone** — mid-epigastric pain radiating to the back, worse supine, relieved leaning forward. Tumor invades celiac plexus. Often misdiagnosed as “mechanical back pain” or chronic pancreatitis.
2. **Acute pancreatitis** — 10% present first with pancreatitis from duct obstruction. No gallstones, no alcohol history → should prompt CT to rule out underlying mass.
3. **Migratory superficial thrombophlebitis (Trousseau’s sign)** — recurrent DVTs/PEs in an unprovoked setting. Pancreatic cancer is a classic hypercoagulable state.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is a malignancy arising from the exocrine ductal epithelium of the pancreas, accounting for >90% of pancreatic cancers. It is characterized by aggressive local invasion, early metastasis, and intense desmoplasia (fibrotic stroma).

**Why it matters:** It is the 4th leading cause of cancer death in the UK, with a 5-year survival of 7–10%. Most patients present at an advanced, unresectable stage because early symptoms are vague or absent. Common clinical pitfalls include attributing painless jaundice to gallstones without imaging, missing Trousseau’s sign, or dismissing new-onset diabetes in an elderly non-obese patient as “type 2” without considering underlying malignancy.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of development:**
Normal pancreatic ductal epithelium → accumulation of mutations (KRAS in >90%, then CDKN2A, TP53, SMAD4) → PanIN (pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia) → invasive carcinoma. The tumor induces a dense desmoplastic reaction (myofibroblasts, collagen) that impairs blood flow and drug delivery → accounts for chemoresistance. This stroma also secretes growth factors (e.g., TGF-β) promoting further invasion.

**Why each key sign happens:**
- **Painless jaundice** — tumor in the pancreatic head compresses the intrapancreatic portion of the common bile duct. Unlike stones, obstruction is gradual; no acute inflammation → no pain. Bile backs up → conjugated bilirubin in blood → yellow skin/sclera, dark urine (bilirubinuria), pale stools (no bile reaching gut).
- **Weight loss/cachexia** — paraneoplastic release of cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) causes catabolism, anorexia, fat loss. Also pancreatic enzyme deficiency → malabsorption → steatorrhea (but may be subtle).
- **Back pain** — tumor invades retroperitoneum and celiac/splanchnic nerve plexus → deep, boring, mid-back pain. Worse lying flat because pancreas moves posteriorly; leaning forward relieves some tension.
- **New-onset diabetes** — tumor secretes islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP) causing insulin resistance, plus destruction of islet cells; often appears before cancer diagnosis by months.
- **Double-duct sign** — obstruction of both common bile duct (CBD) and pancreatic duct (PD) by a head mass → dilation of both ducts proximal to the mass.

**Connect to pattern recognition:** The classic vignette of painless jaundice + palpable gallbladder + weight loss + new diabetes in an older smoker is a direct readout of the pathophys: head mass causing obstructive jaundice and cachexia.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | Most cases – no clear trigger | Elderly patient, no other risk factors |
| **Genetic** | BRCA2 mutation, familial pancreatic cancer (2+ first-degree relatives), hereditary pancreatitis (PRSS1 mutation), Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (STK11 mutation) | Young onset (<50), family history, history of chronic pancreatitis since childhood |
| **Environmental / Lifestyle** | Cigarette smoking (doubles risk), heavy alcohol, obesity, diabetes mellitus (long-standing or recent-onset) | 30+ pack-year smoking; BMI >30; recent diabetes onset |
| **Inflammatory** | Chronic pancreatitis (esp. hereditary and tropical calcific) | Long history of relapsing pancreatitis; calcifications on CT |
| **Occupational** | Exposure to chemicals (e.g., metalworking, pesticides) | Work history (e.g., rubber industry, dry cleaning) |
| **Infection** | H. pylori (weak association), hepatitis B (possible) | Not diagnostic – rarely useful clinically |

**Most common cause:** No single identifiable cause; sporadic >80%. But smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic algorithm (NICE/ESMO):**
First-line: **CT pancreas protocol (triple-phase, thin-cut)**.
If mass seen → **EUS ± FNA** for tissue diagnosis and staging.
If no mass but high suspicion → **EUS** (better for small tumors), **MRCP/ERCP** (if jaundice, can stent).

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|--------------|----------------|
| CT abdomen with pancreas protocol | Hypodense mass in pancreas, ductal dilation, liver metastases, vascular involvement (SMV, SMA, coeliac axis) | Diagnosis + staging (resectable vs borderline vs unresectable) | Mass + double-duct sign = highly suggestive. Vascular encasement >180° = unresectable. |
| EUS ± FNA | High-resolution image of pancreas, regional nodes. FNA yields cytology | Tissue confirmation before chemo/surgery. Better than CT for tumors <2 cm | Positive cytology = confirm PDAC. Negative does not rule out (sampling error). |
| CA 19-9 | Serum tumor marker | Baseline for monitoring response, prognosis. Not diagnostic (elevated in benign jaundice too) | >100 U/mL with mass = strongly suspicious. Rising levels after treatment = progression. |
| LFTs + bilirubin | ALP/GGT raised, bilirubin (conjugated) high | Confirm obstructive jaundice, track response to stenting | Pattern of obstruction. Normal bilirubin does not exclude small head tumors. |
| MRCP / ERCP | Bile/pancreatic duct anatomy, stones | ERCP allows stent placement for palliation; brushings for cytology | Indication for ERCP: jaundice requiring palliation; not routine for diagnosis if CT/EUS available. |
| Staging laparoscopy | Peritoneal metastases (often missed on CT) | Before resecting what appears resectable on CT (up to 25% have occult mets) | Positive → avoid unnecessary laparotomy |

**Pattern recognition:** The **single finding that confirms** diagnosis is positive cytology/histology from EUS-FNA or biopsy. The **single sign that rules out** resectability for cure is >180° encasement of superior mesenteric artery (SMA) or coeliac axis on CT.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) — when patient presents with jaundice or suspected diagnosis:**
- **Resuscitation and assessment of fitness for therapy:** check ECOG PS, comorbidities, nutritional status.
- **Pain management:** if back pain → oral morphine (e.g., morphine sulfate 5–10 mg PO PRN). Avoid NSAIDs in jaundiced patients (risk of acute kidney injury).
- **Biliary drainage if cholangitis:** urgent ERCP with biliary stent + IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV QDS). If not infected but bilirubin >250 µmol/L, consider pre-operative drainage to reduce perioperative risk (controversial; usually only if surgery delayed >2 weeks).
- **DVT prophylaxis:** all patients are hypercoagulable → LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) unless bleeding risk.

**Ward (24–72 hours) — staging and definitive plan:**
- **Staging imaging:** CT pancreas protocol, consider PET-CT if equivocal metastases.
- **Multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion** to decide resectability status.

| Stage | Definition | Management |
|-------|------------|------------|
| **Resectable** | No arterial involvement, SMV/PV <180° | Surgery first (Whipple for head) or neoadjuvant therapy if high risk features. Adjuvant chemotherapy (mFOLFIRINOX or gemcitabine + capecitabine). |
| **Borderline resectable** | SMA ≤180°, SMV/PV >180° but reconstructable | Neoadjuvant chemotherapy (mFOLFIRINOX × 4–6 cycles) then restage + surgery. |
| **Locally advanced (unresectable)** | SMA/coeliac >180°, unreconstructable PV | Chemotherapy (mFOLFIRINOX or gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel). Consider radiation in select cases. |
| **Metastatic** | Liver, peritoneum, distant nodes | Palliative chemotherapy (e.g., gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel or FOLFIRINOX if fit). Best supportive care if ECOG >2). |

- **Chemotherapy regimens (doses):**
  - *mFOLFIRINOX:* oxaliplatin 85 mg/m² IV, irinotecan 180 mg/m² IV, leucovorin 400 mg/m² IV then 5-FU 2400 mg/m² continuous infusion over 46h, every 14 days.
  - *Gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel:* gemcitabine 1000 mg/m² IV day 1+8+15 + nab-paclitaxel 125 mg/m² IV days 1+8+15, every 28 days.
- **Biliary stenting:** if jaundice not drained at ERCP – metal stent preferred for longer patency in malignancy.
- **Nutritional support:** pancreatic enzyme replacement (Creon® 25,000–50,000 units per meal) if steatorrhea. Dietitian referral. Nutritional supplements.

**Long-term:**
- **Pain management:** if persistent, consider celiac plexus neurolysis (under EUS or CT guidance) — reduces opioid burden.
- **Monitor CA19-9 every 2–3 months during chemo.** Rising suggests progression.
- **Surveillance after resection:** cross-sectional imaging every 3–6 months for 2 years, then annually.
- **End-of-life care:** when progression despite all lines, focus on symptom control, advanced care planning.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
- **Do not proceed directly to ERCP for diagnostic purposes without prior CT/EUS** — you will create a false sense of security if no mass seen on CT (small tumors missed).
- **Do not forget to check for resectability criteria before labelling as "palliative"** — up to 20% of initially considered unresectable become resectable after neoadjuvant therapy.
- **Do not give new oral antibiotics for cholangitis without ensuring biliary drainage** — antibiotics alone will not clear obstruction.
- **Do not assume a normal CA19-9 excludes pancreatic cancer** (up to 10–20% are Lewis antigen negative, cannot produce CA19-9).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Pancreatic Cancer | Acute Pancreatitis | Cholangiocarcinoma | Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumor (pNET) |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Dull, epigastric radiating to back; often initially painless | Severe epigastric, radiating to back, abrupt onset | Right upper quadrant pain, often absent | Usually absent or vague; functional tumors (e.g., insulinoma) cause hypoglycemia |
| **Exam** | Icterus, palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier’s), cachexia | Epigastric tenderness, guarding, Grey Turner/Cullen sign | Icterus, hepatomegaly, no palpable gallbladder (gallbladder collapsed from chronic inflammation) | No unique signs; may have hypertension (pheochromocytoma) |
| **CT findings** | Hypodense mass, double-duct sign, vascular encasement | Diffuse gland enlargement, peripancreatic fat stranding, necrosis | Intrahepatic/extrahepatic bile duct thickening, stricture, mass at hilum | Hypervascular enhancing mass in pancreas (different from hypodense PDAC) |
| **Labs** | CA19-9 elevated; bilirubin/ALP high; amylase normal (unless acute on chronic) | Amylase/lipase >3x ULN; CRP high | CA19-9 may be high; also CEA; bilirubin high; amylase normal | No specific marker; may have elevated chromogranin A, insulin, gastrin |
| **Key differentiator** | Double-duct sign + weight loss + new diabetes + smoking history | Severe epigastric pain + high amylase/lipase + no mass | Bile duct stricture with no pancreatic mass; gallstones may be absent | Hypervascular mass on CT with/without hormonal syndrome; slow growth |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**1. Obstructive jaundice with cholangitis:**
- **Presentation:** Rigors, fever, right upper quadrant pain, worsening jaundice.
- **Key signs:** High fever, hypotension, bilirubin rise.
- **What to do:** Urgent ERCP + biliary stent + IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam). Blood cultures. If unstable → sepsis 6 bundle.

**2. Duodenal obstruction:**
- **Presentation:** Early satiety, nausea, vomiting (non-bilious if proximal, bilious if distal).
- **Key signs:** Weight loss continues, abdominal distension, succussion splash.
- **What to do:** Upper GI contrast study, then duodenal stent (palliative) or gastrojejunostomy (if concurrent Whipple possible).

**3. Venous thromboembolism (Trousseau’s syndrome):**
- **Presentation:** Unprovoked DVT (leg swelling, pain) or PE (dyspnoea, hypoxia).
- **Key signs:** Recurrent thrombotic events despite anticoagulation, superficial thrombophlebitis.
- **What to do:** Start LMWH (enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC OD) for minimum 3 months; switch to DOACs after oncology discussion (warfarin less preferred due to variable response).

**4. Cancer cachexia and malnutrition:**
- **Presentation:** Profound weight loss, muscle wasting, fatigue.
- **What to do:** Pancreatic enzyme replacement, nutritional supplements, ± appetite stimulants (megestrol) but limited evidence.

**5. Psychiatric complications:**
- **Depression** is extremely common (up to 50%) – screen with PHQ-9, refer to psycho-oncology. Can impact chemotherapy compliance.

**Late presentation patterns (tested in exams):**
- “An 80-year-old with painless jaundice, no gallbladder palpated” — still suspect pancreatic cancer, but maybe no Courvoisier’s because gallbladder is scarred or tumor above cystic duct.
- “New DVT in a patient with no known cancer” — must trigger CT abdomen to look for pancreatic mass.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**
1. **"Jaundiced Weight Loss Back Pain"** — the triad that screams pancreatic cancer (though not all present this way).
2. **"Courvoisier’s Law + Cholestasis = Ca"** — if you see jaundice + palpable gallbladder, think cancer, not stones.
3. **"Double Duct Sign – Do Diagnose"** — dilation of CBD and PD on CT is highly specific for pancreatic head mass.

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
1. **A new diagnosis of diabetes in a non-obese person over 60 should trigger a CT abdomen** — may be paraneoplastic from pancreatic cancer.
2. **Painless jaundice does not always = painless** — by the time they present, many have mild aching; still suspect cancer.
3. **Courvoisier’s sign is only 50% sensitive** — its absence does not rule out cancer.
4. **Never rely solely on CA19-9 to diagnose** — it is elevated in benign biliary obstruction, pancreatitis, some colon cancers. A value >1000 U/mL is highly concerning for metastatic disease.
5. **Weight loss in pancreatic cancer is disproportionate to the tumor burden** — cachexia is driven by systemic inflammation, not just duct obstruction.
6. **When a patient with known pancreatic cancer develops new ascites** — think peritoneal carcinomatosis, not cirrhosis. Paracentesis may show malignant cells.
7. **The most common site for pancreatic cancer is the head (60-70%)** — thus jaundice is a common presenting sign.

**Common ward mistakes:**
- **Missing Trousseau’s sign** – multiple unprovoked thrombotic events in an elderly smoker → order CT.
- **Starting oral anticoagulation for DVT without imaging for underlying malignancy** – you will treat the clot but miss the cancer.
- **Assuming a head mass is "palliative" because of jaundice** – many head cancers are resectable if no vascular involvement; refer to MDT.
- **Forgetting to prescribe pancreatic enzyme replacement after Whipple surgery** – leads to severe steatorrhea and malnutrition.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call a senior / on-call consultant:**
- **NEWS2 score ≥5** (or any single parameter: RR >24, HR >130, SBP <90, GCS <15, SpO2 <91%).
- **Cholangitis (temperature >38°C, jaundice, rigors)** – needs urgent ERCP and antibiotics.
- **Severe abdominal pain requiring intravenous opioids** – rule out acute pancreatitis, perforation, or biliary obstruction.
- **New-onset confusion or hypoactive delirium** – exclude sepsis, hypercalcemia, hyponatremia (paraneoplastic syndrome).
- **Unstable DVT/PE with haemodynamic instability** – call medical emergency team; may need thrombolysis if massive PE.

**When to consider ICU transfer:**
- **Septic shock** (persistent hypotension after fluid, lactate >4 mmol/L) from cholangitis or post-ERCP pancreatitis.
- **Post-Whipple complications** – haemorrhage (sentinel bleed from pancreaticojejunostomy), anastomotic leak, or pancreatic fistula (drain fluid amylase >3x serum).
- **Acute respiratory failure** from pulmonary embolism or aspiration.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L on ABG** – consider sepsis or bowel ischemia.
- **Bilirubin >250 µmol/L** – discuss pre-operative drainage with surgeon before potential Whipple.
- **CA19-9 increase >50% on two consecutive measurements** – suggests progression; inform oncologist.
- **Weight loss >10% in 3 months** – refer to dietitian and palliative care team early.

rawMarkdown:
## Pancreatic Cancer
**One-liner:** Aggressive ductal adenocarcinoma of the exocrine pancreas; presents late with painless jaundice, weight loss — 5-year survival <10%.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:** A 72-year-old man with a 30-pack-year smoking history and recently diagnosed type 2 diabetes presents with two weeks of progressive jaundice, pruritus, dark urine, and pale stools. He has lost 8 kg unintentionally over three months. On exam, he is icteric with a palpable, non-tender gallbladder (Courvoisier's sign). No fever. LFTs show obstructive picture (bilirubin 120 µmol/L, ALP 450 U/L). CT abdomen reveals a hypodense mass in the pancreatic head with dilated common bile duct and pancreatic duct (“double-duct sign”).

**Dissect the clues:**
- **Painless jaundice + palpable gallbladder** → Courvoisier’s law: in jaundice, a palpable gallbladder is likely malignant (pancreatic cancer) rather than stones (gallbladder is fibrotic/scarred in cholecystitis).
- **Unintentional weight loss** → cachexia from tumor cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) and early satiety from duodenal compression or gastroparesis.
- **New-onset diabetes** → paraneoplastic effect; tumor secretes amylin, causes insulin resistance; also loss of β-cell mass.
- **Smoking history** → strongest modifiable risk factor.
- **Double-duct sign on CT** → pathognomonic for pancreatic head mass obstructing both pancreatic and common bile ducts.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Back pain alone** — mid-epigastric pain radiating to the back, worse supine, relieved leaning forward. Tumor invades celiac plexus. Often misdiagnosed as “mechanical back pain” or chronic pancreatitis.
2. **Acute pancreatitis** — 10% present first with pancreatitis from duct obstruction. No gallstones, no alcohol history → should prompt CT to rule out underlying mass.
3. **Migratory superficial thrombophlebitis (Trousseau’s sign)** — recurrent DVTs/PEs in an unprovoked setting. Pancreatic cancer is a classic hypercoagulable state.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is a malignancy arising from the exocrine ductal epithelium of the pancreas, accounting for >90% of pancreatic cancers. It is characterized by aggressive local invasion, early metastasis, and intense desmoplasia (fibrotic stroma).

**Why it matters:** It is the 4th leading cause of cancer death in the UK, with a 5-year survival of 7–10%. Most patients present at an advanced, unresectable stage because early symptoms are vague or absent. Common clinical pitfalls include attributing painless jaundice to gallstones without imaging, missing Trousseau’s sign, or dismissing new-onset diabetes in an elderly non-obese patient as “type 2” without considering underlying malignancy.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of development:**
Normal pancreatic ductal epithelium → accumulation of mutations (KRAS in >90%, then CDKN2A, TP53, SMAD4) → PanIN (pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia) → invasive carcinoma. The tumor induces a dense desmoplastic reaction (myofibroblasts, collagen) that impairs blood flow and drug delivery → accounts for chemoresistance. This stroma also secretes growth factors (e.g., TGF-β) promoting further invasion.

**Why each key sign happens:**
- **Painless jaundice** — tumor in the pancreatic head compresses the intrapancreatic portion of the common bile duct. Unlike stones, obstruction is gradual; no acute inflammation → no pain. Bile backs up → conjugated bilirubin in blood → yellow skin/sclera, dark urine (bilirubinuria), pale stools (no bile reaching gut).
- **Weight loss/cachexia** — paraneoplastic release of cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) causes catabolism, anorexia, fat loss. Also pancreatic enzyme deficiency → malabsorption → steatorrhea (but may be subtle).
- **Back pain** — tumor invades retroperitoneum and celiac/splanchnic nerve plexus → deep, boring, mid-back pain. Worse lying flat because pancreas moves posteriorly; leaning forward relieves some tension.
- **New-onset diabetes** — tumor secretes islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP) causing insulin resistance, plus destruction of islet cells; often appears before cancer diagnosis by months.
- **Double-duct sign** — obstruction of both common bile duct (CBD) and pancreatic duct (PD) by a head mass → dilation of both ducts proximal to the mass.

**Connect to pattern recognition:** The classic vignette of painless jaundice + palpable gallbladder + weight loss + new diabetes in an older smoker is a direct readout of the pathophys: head mass causing obstructive jaundice and cachexia.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | Most cases – no clear trigger | Elderly patient, no other risk factors |
| **Genetic** | BRCA2 mutation, familial pancreatic cancer (2+ first-degree relatives), hereditary pancreatitis (PRSS1 mutation), Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (STK11 mutation) | Young onset (<50), family history, history of chronic pancreatitis since childhood |
| **Environmental / Lifestyle** | Cigarette smoking (doubles risk), heavy alcohol, obesity, diabetes mellitus (long-standing or recent-onset) | 30+ pack-year smoking; BMI >30; recent diabetes onset |
| **Inflammatory** | Chronic pancreatitis (esp. hereditary and tropical calcific) | Long history of relapsing pancreatitis; calcifications on CT |
| **Occupational** | Exposure to chemicals (e.g., metalworking, pesticides) | Work history (e.g., rubber industry, dry cleaning) |
| **Infection** | H. pylori (weak association), hepatitis B (possible) | Not diagnostic – rarely useful clinically |

**Most common cause:** No single identifiable cause; sporadic >80%. But smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic algorithm (NICE/ESMO):**
First-line: **CT pancreas protocol (triple-phase, thin-cut)**.
If mass seen → **EUS ± FNA** for tissue diagnosis and staging.
If no mass but high suspicion → **EUS** (better for small tumors), **MRCP/ERCP** (if jaundice, can stent).

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|--------------|----------------|
| CT abdomen with pancreas protocol | Hypodense mass in pancreas, ductal dilation, liver metastases, vascular involvement (SMV, SMA, coeliac axis) | Diagnosis + staging (resectable vs borderline vs unresectable) | Mass + double-duct sign = highly suggestive. Vascular encasement >180° = unresectable. |
| EUS ± FNA | High-resolution image of pancreas, regional nodes. FNA yields cytology | Tissue confirmation before chemo/surgery. Better than CT for tumors <2 cm | Positive cytology = confirm PDAC. Negative does not rule out (sampling error). |
| CA 19-9 | Serum tumor marker | Baseline for monitoring response, prognosis. Not diagnostic (elevated in benign jaundice too) | >100 U/mL with mass = strongly suspicious. Rising levels after treatment = progression. |
| LFTs + bilirubin | ALP/GGT raised, bilirubin (conjugated) high | Confirm obstructive jaundice, track response to stenting | Pattern of obstruction. Normal bilirubin does not exclude small head tumors. |
| MRCP / ERCP | Bile/pancreatic duct anatomy, stones | ERCP allows stent placement for palliation; brushings for cytology | Indication for ERCP: jaundice requiring palliation; not routine for diagnosis if CT/EUS available. |
| Staging laparoscopy | Peritoneal metastases (often missed on CT) | Before resecting what appears resectable on CT (up to 25% have occult mets) | Positive → avoid unnecessary laparotomy |

**Pattern recognition:** The **single finding that confirms** diagnosis is positive cytology/histology from EUS-FNA or biopsy. The **single sign that rules out** resectability for cure is >180° encasement of superior mesenteric artery (SMA) or coeliac axis on CT.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) — when patient presents with jaundice or suspected diagnosis:**
- **Resuscitation and assessment of fitness for therapy:** check ECOG PS, comorbidities, nutritional status.
- **Pain management:** if back pain → oral morphine (e.g., morphine sulfate 5–10 mg PO PRN). Avoid NSAIDs in jaundiced patients (risk of acute kidney injury).
- **Biliary drainage if cholangitis:** urgent ERCP with biliary stent + IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV QDS). If not infected but bilirubin >250 µmol/L, consider pre-operative drainage to reduce perioperative risk (controversial; usually only if surgery delayed >2 weeks).
- **DVT prophylaxis:** all patients are hypercoagulable → LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) unless bleeding risk.

**Ward (24–72 hours) — staging and definitive plan:**
- **Staging imaging:** CT pancreas protocol, consider PET-CT if equivocal metastases.
- **Multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion** to decide resectability status.

| Stage | Definition | Management |
|-------|------------|------------|
| **Resectable** | No arterial involvement, SMV/PV <180° | Surgery first (Whipple for head) or neoadjuvant therapy if high risk features. Adjuvant chemotherapy (mFOLFIRINOX or gemcitabine + capecitabine). |
| **Borderline resectable** | SMA ≤180°, SMV/PV >180° but reconstructable | Neoadjuvant chemotherapy (mFOLFIRINOX × 4–6 cycles) then restage + surgery. |
| **Locally advanced (unresectable)** | SMA/coeliac >180°, unreconstructable PV | Chemotherapy (mFOLFIRINOX or gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel). Consider radiation in select cases. |
| **Metastatic** | Liver, peritoneum, distant nodes | Palliative chemotherapy (e.g., gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel or FOLFIRINOX if fit). Best supportive care if ECOG >2). |

- **Chemotherapy regimens (doses):**
  - *mFOLFIRINOX:* oxaliplatin 85 mg/m² IV, irinotecan 180 mg/m² IV, leucovorin 400 mg/m² IV then 5-FU 2400 mg/m² continuous infusion over 46h, every 14 days.
  - *Gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel:* gemcitabine 1000 mg/m² IV day 1+8+15 + nab-paclitaxel 125 mg/m² IV days 1+8+15, every 28 days.
- **Biliary stenting:** if jaundice not drained at ERCP – metal stent preferred for longer patency in malignancy.
- **Nutritional support:** pancreatic enzyme replacement (Creon® 25,000–50,000 units per meal) if steatorrhea. Dietitian referral. Nutritional supplements.

**Long-term:**
- **Pain management:** if persistent, consider celiac plexus neurolysis (under EUS or CT guidance) — reduces opioid burden.
- **Monitor CA19-9 every 2–3 months during chemo.** Rising suggests progression.
- **Surveillance after resection:** cross-sectional imaging every 3–6 months for 2 years, then annually.
- **End-of-life care:** when progression despite all lines, focus on symptom control, advanced care planning.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
- **Do not proceed directly to ERCP for diagnostic purposes without prior CT/EUS** — you will create a false sense of security if no mass seen on CT (small tumors missed).
- **Do not forget to check for resectability criteria before labelling as "palliative"** — up to 20% of initially considered unresectable become resectable after neoadjuvant therapy.
- **Do not give new oral antibiotics for cholangitis without ensuring biliary drainage** — antibiotics alone will not clear obstruction.
- **Do not assume a normal CA19-9 excludes pancreatic cancer** (up to 10–20% are Lewis antigen negative, cannot produce CA19-9).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Pancreatic Cancer | Acute Pancreatitis | Cholangiocarcinoma | Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumor (pNET) |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Dull, epigastric radiating to back; often initially painless | Severe epigastric, radiating to back, abrupt onset | Right upper quadrant pain, often absent | Usually absent or vague; functional tumors (e.g., insulinoma) cause hypoglycemia |
| **Exam** | Icterus, palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier’s), cachexia | Epigastric tenderness, guarding, Grey Turner/Cullen sign | Icterus, hepatomegaly, no palpable gallbladder (gallbladder collapsed from chronic inflammation) | No unique signs; may have hypertension (pheochromocytoma) |
| **CT findings** | Hypodense mass, double-duct sign, vascular encasement | Diffuse gland enlargement, peripancreatic fat stranding, necrosis | Intrahepatic/extrahepatic bile duct thickening, stricture, mass at hilum | Hypervascular enhancing mass in pancreas (different from hypodense PDAC) |
| **Labs** | CA19-9 elevated; bilirubin/ALP high; amylase normal (unless acute on chronic) | Amylase/lipase >3x ULN; CRP high | CA19-9 may be high; also CEA; bilirubin high; amylase normal | No specific marker; may have elevated chromogranin A, insulin, gastrin |
| **Key differentiator** | Double-duct sign + weight loss + new diabetes + smoking history | Severe epigastric pain + high amylase/lipase + no mass | Bile duct stricture with no pancreatic mass; gallstones may be absent | Hypervascular mass on CT with/without hormonal syndrome; slow growth |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**1. Obstructive jaundice with cholangitis:**
- **Presentation:** Rigors, fever, right upper quadrant pain, worsening jaundice.
- **Key signs:** High fever, hypotension, bilirubin rise.
- **What to do:** Urgent ERCP + biliary stent + IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam). Blood cultures. If unstable → sepsis 6 bundle.

**2. Duodenal obstruction:**
- **Presentation:** Early satiety, nausea, vomiting (non-bilious if proximal, bilious if distal).
- **Key signs:** Weight loss continues, abdominal distension, succussion splash.
- **What to do:** Upper GI contrast study, then duodenal stent (palliative) or gastrojejunostomy (if concurrent Whipple possible).

**3. Venous thromboembolism (Trousseau’s syndrome):**
- **Presentation:** Unprovoked DVT (leg swelling, pain) or PE (dyspnoea, hypoxia).
- **Key signs:** Recurrent thrombotic events despite anticoagulation, superficial thrombophlebitis.
- **What to do:** Start LMWH (enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC OD) for minimum 3 months; switch to DOACs after oncology discussion (warfarin less preferred due to variable response).

**4. Cancer cachexia and malnutrition:**
- **Presentation:** Profound weight loss, muscle wasting, fatigue.
- **What to do:** Pancreatic enzyme replacement, nutritional supplements, ± appetite stimulants (megestrol) but limited evidence.

**5. Psychiatric complications:**
- **Depression** is extremely common (up to 50%) – screen with PHQ-9, refer to psycho-oncology. Can impact chemotherapy compliance.

**Late presentation patterns (tested in exams):**
- “An 80-year-old with painless jaundice, no gallbladder palpated” — still suspect pancreatic cancer, but maybe no Courvoisier’s because gallbladder is scarred or tumor above cystic duct.
- “New DVT in a patient with no known cancer” — must trigger CT abdomen to look for pancreatic mass.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**
1. **"Jaundiced Weight Loss Back Pain"** — the triad that screams pancreatic cancer (though not all present this way).
2. **"Courvoisier’s Law + Cholestasis = Ca"** — if you see jaundice + palpable gallbladder, think cancer, not stones.
3. **"Double Duct Sign – Do Diagnose"** — dilation of CBD and PD on CT is highly specific for pancreatic head mass.

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
1. **A new diagnosis of diabetes in a non-obese person over 60 should trigger a CT abdomen** — may be paraneoplastic from pancreatic cancer.
2. **Painless jaundice does not always = painless** — by the time they present, many have mild aching; still suspect cancer.
3. **Courvoisier’s sign is only 50% sensitive** — its absence does not rule out cancer.
4. **Never rely solely on CA19-9 to diagnose** — it is elevated in benign biliary obstruction, pancreatitis, some colon cancers. A value >1000 U/mL is highly concerning for metastatic disease.
5. **Weight loss in pancreatic cancer is disproportionate to the tumor burden** — cachexia is driven by systemic inflammation, not just duct obstruction.
6. **When a patient with known pancreatic cancer develops new ascites** — think peritoneal carcinomatosis, not cirrhosis. Paracentesis may show malignant cells.
7. **The most common site for pancreatic cancer is the head (60-70%)** — thus jaundice is a common presenting sign.

**Common ward mistakes:**
- **Missing Trousseau’s sign** – multiple unprovoked thrombotic events in an elderly smoker → order CT.
- **Starting oral anticoagulation for DVT without imaging for underlying malignancy** – you will treat the clot but miss the cancer.
- **Assuming a head mass is "palliative" because of jaundice** – many head cancers are resectable if no vascular involvement; refer to MDT.
- **Forgetting to prescribe pancreatic enzyme replacement after Whipple surgery** – leads to severe steatorrhea and malnutrition.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call a senior / on-call consultant:**
- **NEWS2 score ≥5** (or any single parameter: RR >24, HR >130, SBP <90, GCS <15, SpO2 <91%).
- **Cholangitis (temperature >38°C, jaundice, rigors)** – needs urgent ERCP and antibiotics.
- **Severe abdominal pain requiring intravenous opioids** – rule out acute pancreatitis, perforation, or biliary obstruction.
- **New-onset confusion or hypoactive delirium** – exclude sepsis, hypercalcemia, hyponatremia (paraneoplastic syndrome).
- **Unstable DVT/PE with haemodynamic instability** – call medical emergency team; may need thrombolysis if massive PE.

**When to consider ICU transfer:**
- **Septic shock** (persistent hypotension after fluid, lactate >4 mmol/L) from cholangitis or post-ERCP pancreatitis.
- **Post-Whipple complications** – haemorrhage (sentinel bleed from pancreaticojejunostomy), anastomotic leak, or pancreatic fistula (drain fluid amylase >3x serum).
- **Acute respiratory failure** from pulmonary embolism or aspiration.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L on ABG** – consider sepsis or bowel ischemia.
- **Bilirubin >250 µmol/L** – discuss pre-operative drainage with surgeon before potential Whipple.
- **CA19-9 increase >50% on two consecutive measurements** – suggests progression; inform oncologist.
- **Weight loss >10% in 3 months** – refer to dietitian and palliative care team early.
